mnemonics.title: Types of Capacity Planning
mnemonics.content:
STOP - Strategic (long), Tactical (medium), Operational (short)

S - Strategic (Years)
T - Tactical (Months)
O - Operational (Days/Weeks)

mnemonics.title: Capacity Planning Process Steps
mnemonics.content:
M A C R O
M - Monitor (collect data)
A - Analyze (identify trends)
C - Compare (vs baselines/SLAs)
R - Recommend (solutions)
O - Optimize (implement changes)

mnemonics.title: Key Capacity Metrics
mnemonics.content:
T-U-R-F
T - Throughput (work per time)
U - Utilisation (capacity in use)
R - Response Time (speed)
F - Failure Rate (reliability)

acronyms.usage: Defines performance benchmarks that capacity planning must ensure are met under various load conditions
Used to justify capacity investments by comparing benefits to costs of provisioning resources
Metrics used to measure capacity planning effectiveness like utilisation, throughput, response time
Repository containing details of IT assets used for capacity planning analysis and impact assessment
Set of performance standards that capacity planning must maintain, including bandwidth, latency, reliability

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a bathtub: as water (load) fills, initially no overflow (good performance). At 80% full, water starts spilling (performance drops). The curve is S-shaped - flat at extremes, steep in middle. This shows why 70-80% utilization is optimal threshold.
Draw a horizontal line showing time: far left = Operational (days/weeks), middle = Tactical (months), far right = Strategic (years). Each type addresses different planning horizon and detail level.
Vertical scaling = adding floors to a building (existing foundation). Horizontal scaling = building more houses in a neighborhood. Both increase capacity but differently.
Think of seating in a theater: under-provisioning = people standing (service failure), over-provisioning = empty seats (wasted cost). Right capacity = full but not crowded.

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant managing seats: baseline = normal dinner service, peak = Friday night (needs reservations/extra staff), forecast = holiday bookings. Too few tables = angry customers, too many = wasted rent.
Highway capacity: adding lanes = horizontal scaling, widening existing lanes = vertical scaling. Traffic jams = performance degradation. Toll collection = load balancing to manage flow.
Baseline is like your thermostat setting - the normal comfortable temperature. Deviations trigger action: too hot (overload) or too cold (idle capacity). You monitor to maintain consistency.
Dashboard warning lights (low fuel, high temperature) are like capacity thresholds - they alert you before crisis, allowing proactive response rather than emergency breakdown.
